ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pig meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for Korean-style hot plate BBQ pork exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Korean BBQ pork preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of hot plate BBQ pork is approximately 250 g, representing a single-plate restaurant portion as typically served in Korean restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, broiled (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 10062); adapted with Korean BBQ marinade adjustment from USDA Foundation Foods pork belly and shoulder entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pork (loin and belly cuts) to reflect a typical Korean BBQ pork preparation. Korean BBQ marinades (soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic) contribute additional sodium and sugar; sodium is adjusted upward to approximately 600 mg/100 g to reflect marinade absorption. Energy, protein, and fat are anchored to USDA cooked pork belly/shoulder values. This is an estimated composite; no exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for Korean hot plate BBQ pork was identified.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — meat and protein-rich foods with marinade carbohydrate contribution; analogous estimate based on grilled marinated pork (GI ~35) from Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean hot plate BBQ pork. GI estimated at 35 based on grilled marinated pork analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). Pork is a protein- and fat-dominant food; the small carbohydrate contribution from marinade (sugar, soy sauce) is estimated to yield a low GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.55, rounded to 4.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce-based marinade (~600 mg/100 g; ~1500 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Korean BBQ pork due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning marinades; a single 250 g serving may provide approximately 1500 mg sodium, representing 65% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) but moderate fat content and added sugars in marinade
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot plate BBQ pork has a low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~4.6), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management; however, the added sugars in the marinade and high fat content warrant portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~5.9 g/100 g) and high sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~5.9 g/100 g) and high sodium load (~600 mg/100 g) in Korean BBQ pork are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, and prefer leaner pork cuts when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Plate BBQ Pork (石板烤猪肉; Korean: 철판 바비큐 돼지고기) is a Korean-style grilled pork dish served sizzling on a hot stone or iron plate. Typically prepared using pork belly or shoulder, the meat is marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, sugar, and sometimes gochujang (Korean chilli paste), then grilled at high heat to achieve caramelised, slightly charred edges with a tender, juicy interior. The dish is a staple of Korean barbecue cuisine and is popular in Korean restaurants across Singapore, where it is often served as a main course alongside steamed rice, kimchi, and banchan (Korean side dishes). Nutritionally, it is a protein-rich dish (~17.5 g protein per 100 g) with moderate to high fat content (~16.8 g fat per 100 g), including saturated fat (~5.9 g/100 g). The soy sauce-based marinade contributes significant sodium (~600 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (~35) due to the protein and fat dominance, with a modest carbohydrate contribution from marinade sugars (~5.2 g/100 g). It is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. Clinically, portion control is advised for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: What is hot plate BBQ pork?
How many calories in Korean BBQ pork?
Is hot plate pork high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat Korean BBQ pork?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was identified for Korean hot plate BBQ pork. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pork entries with marinade sodium and sugar adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked pork (loin, belly, shoulder) entries adapted to reflect Korean BBQ marinade composition. Sodium is adjusted upward to ~600 mg/100 g to account for soy sauce marinade absorption. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy pork entries used for this adaptation. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats in pork; this is not an industrial trans fat. GI is an analogous estimate (~35) based on grilled marinated pork from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Korean hot plate BBQ pork was found. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (35 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.55, rounded to 4.6. This dish is served at Mincheng Bibimbap (민정비빔밥 敏丁拌) at Funan, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data (GD003911). The food is not halal-certified as pork is a non-halal ingredient. Korean cuisine multilingual requirement satisfied with Korean native-script name (철판 바비큐 돼지고기). Chinese names (石板烤猪肉 / 石板烤豬肉) are preserved from the original input record.
